title: Fill Yourself with the Spirit
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Fill Yourself with the Spirit - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 5_17-21.txt

outline.title: The Holy Spirit in Ephesians
subpoints: The Spirit seals believers with the guarantee of eternal life (Eph. 1:13-14)
The Spirit gives access to the Father in prayer (Eph. 2:18; 6:18)
The Spirit reveals the mystery of the gospel to the church (Eph. 3:5-6)
The Spirit strengthens believers at the core of who they are (Eph. 3:16)
The Spirit unites the church in the bond of peace (Eph. 4:3-4)
The Spirit equips the church with her only offensive weapon — the Word (Eph. 6:17)

outline.title: Being Filled with the Spirit Is Active, Not Passive
subpoints: A believer always has the Spirit; being filled is not a second experience
Paul commands us to fill ourselves — it is active obedience, not passive reception
The participles in verses 19-21 describe the manner of being filled
Being filled is not a subjective 'zapping' but a life of purposeful obedience

outline.title: Four Activities That Fill Us with the Spirit
subpoints: Speaking to one another in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs — corporate singing
Singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord — internal, meditative worship
Giving thanks always for all things to God the Father through Christ — gratitude
Submitting to one another in the fear of God — mutual humble submission

outline.title: The Necessity and Meaning of Corporate Singing
subpoints: The Bible has no category for a Christian who does not sing
Christians sing in joy and in despair — Psalm 77, 88, 137 are not happy songs
Singing is outward (to one another) and inward (from the heart to God)
Meditation — filling the mind with God's Word — is the opposite of emptying the mind

outline.title: Mutual Submission: What It Looks Like
subpoints: The basis for submitting to others is reverence for Christ, not the merit of others
Submission requires humility — esteeming others better than yourself (Phil. 2:3-4)
Submission means giving of yourself to others, not requiring others to submit to you
Practical ways: do good to others, serve those near you, receive godly correction, pray for one another
Being filled with the Spirit is a corporate pursuit — it requires community

summary:
In this sermon on Ephesians 5:17-21, Pastor Tito Lyro surveys the Holy Spirit's ministry throughout the book of Ephesians before focusing on the command to be filled with the Spirit. He argues that being filled is not a passive, subjective experience but an active, obedient way of life described by the four participles that follow Paul's command: speaking to one another in song, making melody in the heart, giving thanks, and submitting to one another.

The sermon challenges a common misunderstanding that being filled with the Spirit manifests in dramatic supernatural acts. Paul locates the Spirit-filled life instead in the ordinary practices of corporate worship, personal meditation on God's Word, gratitude in all circumstances, and humble mutual submission. Pastor Lyro notes that this means being filled with the Spirit is fundamentally a communal, corporate affair — it requires the presence and participation of the body of Christ.

The final section focuses on mutual submission, explaining that it is grounded not in the worthiness of the people around us but in reverence for Christ. Drawing on Philippians 2, the sermon holds up Christ's own self-emptying as the pattern for submission. Practical expressions of submission include doing good to those near you, receiving godly correction with humility, and praying for one another.

The sermon was delivered in the context of COVID-era restrictions, with the pastor noting that congregational singing had been criminalized by government order, making the exhortation to sing together an act of conscientious defiance rooted in the core identity of the Christian community.
transcript:
## Standing for the Word

If you're able, you remain standing. And once again, turn to Ephesians chapter 5 and your Bibles. Ephesians 5, and we'll go to read verses 17 through 21 of Ephesians chapter 5. So here is the word of our Lord.

> Therefore, do not be unwise but understand what the will of the Lord is. And do not be drunk with wine in which is dissipation. But be filled with the Spirit, speaking to one another in Psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord, giving thanks always for all things to God the Father, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, submitting to one another in the fear of God.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together.

Father, we approach your word this morning, the Zion to hear from you. We pray that you would open our hearts to see your goodness in this passage. We pray in Jesus name. Amen. Please be seated.

## The Holy Spirit's Ministry in Ephesians

The Holy Spirit of God is the executor of all the plans of the Godhead. He is the one who applies the work of the Son, accomplishing his life and his death and his resurrection. The Holy Spirit is the doer. The Father plans, the Son executes, the Spirit applies the work to people. He is the one who is working in the elect of God. He is the one who is regenerating dead hearts even today. He is in charge of our growth in the Lord. He is in charge of our sanctification.

So the Holy Spirit is essential for the ministry of the Church. A church cannot exist apart from the work of the Holy Spirit in its midst. The Church cannot be effective in discipling nations unless the Spirit of God is working through it in the world.

So we should find many references to the Holy Spirit in a letter such as Ephesians whose main focus and main theme is the Church of Jesus Christ. Ephesians is not only the letter about the Church of Jesus Christ, but also the letter about the Spirit of Jesus Christ as the Spirit works through the Church of Jesus Christ.

So I would like to begin by taking you on a brief journey through Ephesians and looking at how Paul speaks of the Holy Spirit and his ministry in the Church of Jesus Christ. Then I want us to take a look at the specific passage that we are considering this morning and the idea that we have to be filled with the Spirit — or better yet, we are to fill ourselves with the Spirit — and the four things that Paul tells us to do to be filled with the Spirit. And then I want us to finish by spending some time on the fourth thing, the idea that we're to submit ourselves to one another as a way that we are filled by the Spirit.

### The Holy Spirit Seals Us

Look at chapter 1, verses 13 and 14:

> In him, you also trusted after you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation; and in whom also, having believed, you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise, who is the guarantee of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession, to the praise of his glory.

The Spirit of God assures us that Christ is coming back and that we have eternal life and that we will see him as he is, for we shall be like him.

### The Holy Spirit Gives Us Access to the Father

Look at chapter 2, verse 18: *For through him we both have access by one Spirit to the Father.* He repeats that in chapter 6 verse 18 — the Spirit is the one who brings our prayers to the Father.

### The Holy Spirit Reveals the Mystery of the Gospel

Chapter 3, verses 5 and 6: the Holy Spirit tells us that all nations will be blessed in Jesus Christ, that all nations will be grafted into Israel, the Church of Jesus. *Which in other ages was not made known to the sons of man as it has now been revealed by the Spirit, to his holy apostles and prophets — that the Gentiles should be fellow heirs of the same body and partakers of his promise in Christ through the gospel.*

### The Holy Spirit Strengthens the Inner Man

Look at verse 16 of chapter 3: *That he would grant you, according to the riches of his glory, to be strengthened with might through his Spirit in the inner man.* It is the Spirit of God who strengthens us at the core of who we are to follow God, to do what God commands, to believe in God, to do the things that God calls us to do.

### The Holy Spirit Unites the Church

Look at chapter 4 verses 3 and 4: *Endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. There is one body and one Spirit, just as you are called in one hope of your calling.*

### The Holy Spirit Arms the Church

And the Holy Spirit gives the church her only offensive weapon in the war that we are engaged in — the war against the principalities and powers. Of all the things in the armor of God, there is only one offensive weapon. What is it? It's the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God.

So the Spirit strengthens and gives the church her offensive weapon to fight the battle that God calls her to fight. And it is this Spirit that we must be filled with.

## Being Filled with the Spirit

Now a believer always has the Spirit in him. That's not what it means to be filled with the Spirit. The Spirit is already with you if you're a believer. But to be filled with the Spirit is to believe, do, and say the very things that the Spirit guides you to do.

One thing that is important to keep in mind is that being filled with the Spirit is not merely a subjective experience. In the twentieth century, bleeding into the twenty-first, we've been told that being filled with the Spirit is some sort of passive thing that happens to you — very subjective — and that you have really no participation in it. People think of the filling of the Spirit as a kind of passive zapping. That is not how the Bible teaches the filling of the Spirit happens.

Notice that Paul commands us to be filled — or to fill ourselves — with the Spirit. If you look at verses 19 to 21 of our passage, these participles ending in *-ing* are all describing the command to be filled with the Spirit. In other words, in the context of Ephesians, one is filled with the Spirit as one does these things that Paul lists in verses 19 through 21. So we have four activities listed here that mark the Spirit-filled life.

## The Four Activities of the Spirit-Filled Life

### 1. Speaking to One Another in Psalms, Hymns, and Spiritual Songs

The very first description of being filled with the Spirit has not an inward focus but an outward focus. *Speaking to one another* — the Bible tells us that being filled with the Spirit has a lot to do with other people in your life. This is an allusion to the corporate worship of God. Spirit-filled people will sing together for the glory of God and the good of one another.

The Bible does not have a category for the Christian who doesn't sing. The Bible's authors just cannot think about that. And the reason we have a difficult time with that is that we always try to equate singing with being happy. The Bible doesn't do that. Read the book of Psalms. Read Psalm 77. Read Psalm 88. Read Psalm 137. They're not happy, slappy songs. They are the songs of those in despair. So the Christian sings when he is happy. The Christian sings when he is in despair, because that's what Christians do.

Carl Truman wrote an essay called "What Shall Miserable Christians Sing" — an argument for singing the psalms. Did you know that there are Christians who are miserable? Of course there are. And we've bought this lie that somehow in church everybody's supposed to be happy and sing these happy songs. That's not the Christianity of the Bible. The Bible's Christians sometimes struggle, sometimes are miserable, are in despair. And even from the depths of the pit they sing. Psalm 40 is an excellent example — David says that from the miry pit, he sang, and God rescued him.

### 2. Singing and Making Melody in Your Heart to the Lord

Look at verse 19: *singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord.* The Christian not only sings aloud outwardly to the glory of God and to bless others around him; he also sings from his or her heart. It's not just an outward observation — it's also a heart issue. That speaking is coming out of a heart that is singing to the Lord. Your heart is focused on the Lord. You are talking to yourself as well as to the Lord.

Psalm 42 and 43 are a great example of this — the Psalmist not only talks to others, not only talks to God, but also talks to himself: *Why are you so disquieted within me, my soul? Hope in God.* This implies a constant and conscientious state of mind, purposeful thinking. You are filled with the Spirit when your heart is meditating on the Lord.

The Puritans were great on this. They would read their Bible, perhaps memorize a passage or verse, and then take time to think about that verse — how it applied, what it meant — turning it over in their minds from every angle. Biblical meditation is the opposite of what people call meditation today. Transcendental meditation empties the mind. Biblical meditation fills the mind with God and his Word.

### 3. Giving Thanks Always for All Things

Verse 20: *giving thanks always for all things to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.* One cannot live a life of ingratitude and be filled with the Spirit. The Bible has no category for an ungrateful Christian. The Christian has no reason to be ungrateful regardless of circumstances, and we fill the Spirit when we have an active heart of thanksgiving.

Interestingly, people want the feeling of the Spirit to manifest itself in speaking in strange languages or healing the sick or raising the dead. That's how they think: I am filled with the Spirit, I do all these mighty, powerful, self-exalting acts. And here Paul says, actually, you are filled with the Spirit when you are simply thankful. But pastor, that's too hard! I'd prefer to raise the dead. Yes — exactly the point. You want all these self-exalting acts. But the simple act of thankfulness to God through Jesus Christ — that is where the Spirit is.

This thankfulness is not aimless. It is directed to God through Jesus Christ. We're not just called to be thankful in some vague way — we're called to be thankful to God through Jesus Christ.

### 4. Submitting to One Another in the Fear of God

Verse 21: *submitting to one another in the fear of God.* Again, being filled with the Spirit according to the Scriptures looks a lot humbler than what many people say it looks like. Those who are filled with the Spirit submit themselves to one another.

Do you find it interesting that Paul doesn't describe being filled with the Spirit with great feats or great conquests, but equates it with being humbly submissive to one another? Paul defines what submitting to one another looks like in Philippians 2:3-4: *Let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself. Let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others.*

I want the power of the Spirit. I want the Spirit to be magnified in my life. I want to be filled by the Spirit. Then humbly submit yourself to others. Think of the interests of others ahead of your own. And I'm going to commit a cultural sin right now: do not be self-assertive. That is how being filled with the Spirit looks.

And notice that being filled with the Holy Spirit is a corporate pursuit. You can't really sing and speak to people if they're not there. You can't really submit to people if they're not there. It is impossible to be filled by the Spirit by yourself. This passage in Ephesians depends on having people around you. People, you need each other. We need each other. If we're going to be filled by the power of the Spirit, we need each other.

## Why Submit to One Another?

**Reason 1: Because we are new creatures.** We've been transformed by the Spirit of God. Tracing the *therefore* and *for* back through chapter 5 and into chapter 4, we arrive at 4:20-24, where Paul tells us that we are to submit to one another because we have been saved, transformed, translated from the kingdom of darkness to the kingdom of God's Son. God has enabled us to shed pride and put on the humility needed to submit to another.

**Reason 2: Because we are to walk carefully.** Submitting to one another is part of our Christian walk. One of the tests of the genuineness of your Christianity is whether you humbly submit to others. The author of *Invictus* — "I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul" — if you subscribe to that, you are not a Christian. The Christian essentially submits to another.

**Reason 3: Because the days are evil.** In verses 15-16, Paul says we are to walk carefully, redeeming the time, because the days we live in are evil. We redeem the time and do good by submitting to one another. Jesus himself said the world will see we are his disciples if we love one another, and part of that love is humble submission.

**Reason 4: Because this is how we become filled with the Spirit.** Not by asserting our rights every time, but by humbling ourselves before God and submitting to one another.

## What Mutual Submission Looks Like

First, notice that submission has nothing to do with the people around you. You are not submitting to somebody because they are worthy. Look again at verse 21: *submitting to one another in the fear of God.* The basis of mutual submission is reverence for Christ — not what others have done, but who Christ is.

Mutual submission requires humility — Paul says in Philippians 2:3, *let nothing be done through selfish ambition or conceit, but in lowliness of mind let each esteem others better than himself.* F. F. Bruce, commenting on Philippians 2:3, says: Christians should not be self-assertive, each insisting on getting his or her own way. They should be humble enough to count others better than themselves and put the interests of others before their own.

Mutual submission requires giving of ourselves to others — *let each of you look out not only for his own interests, but also for the interests of others* (Phil. 2:4). And how does Paul illustrate that? By pointing to Christ, who, being God himself, did not think that being God should keep him from taking upon himself flesh like ours and humbling himself to the death of the cross. That is the pattern of mutual submission. And Paul also gives himself as an example: *For we do not preach ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your bond-servants for Jesus' sake* (2 Cor. 4:5).

When we read verse 21, our first thought should not be: *How do I get people to submit to me?* Our first thought must be: *How can I give of myself to others and humbly serve them?*

Mutual submission also requires serving others. Jesus washed his disciples' feet and said, *You have seen what I have done for you — now go and do the same.*

## Practical Ways to Submit and Serve

1. Take every opportunity you have to do good for the people around you. Look for ways to bless them. *Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due, when it is in the power of your hand to do so* (Proverbs 3:27).

2. Look for ways to humbly serve the people who are close to you. It is easy to raise money to build an orphanage for a hundred kids in Mali or Kenya. It is very difficult to get someone to go visit someone sitting right next to them in the pew. God has placed you where you are to submit and serve the people around you.

3. Take godly review to heart. Pride does not listen to others. Pride only promotes itself. But humility will welcome godly advice.

4. Pray for one another. John Bunyan said, *You can do more than pray after you have prayed. But you cannot do more than pray until you have prayed.* Think about the people around you right now. When was the last time you prayed for that person? Pray for one another.

Being filled with the Spirit has everything to do with how you serve and relate to other people. That is where the focus is. So Paul says in verse 18: don't seek fulfillment in substance — all that is is selfishness. Fill yourself with the Holy Spirit of God and serve one another.

## Closing Prayer

Father, we pray that you empower us with your Spirit that we might be able to serve one another, that we might be able to sing to one another, that we might be thankful in our hearts, that we might be filled with your Spirit. Humble us to be more like the Lord Jesus Christ who was willing to be humbled for our sake. We thank you that you have exalted him. And we thank you that you tell us that if we humble ourselves, you will lift us up. We pray all these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
